personSummary.value: Walks slowly but steadily with a walker during daytime routines. Usually accepts evening medication when a familiar nurse explains the sequence first. Often rests after lunch and prefers unhurried transitions. Prefers the room door partly closed during evening care. Responds well to warm tea after meals. Likes hearing that her daughter Mika was updated when family context is relevant. Daughter Mika is the primary family contact and usually visits on Sunday afternoons. Family reports that quiet music after dinner has helped with settling in the past.

value.description: Frequent evening medication refusals linked to noisier environments.

citations.quote: Aiko refused evening medication again around 19:40 when the corridor was noisy after the fire drill.
Resident again refused medication and repeated, 'I do not want it tonight.'
Aiko refused evening medication again around 19:40 when the corridor was noisy after the fire drill.
Resident again refused medication and repeated, 'I do not want it tonight.'

value: Lower room noise, sit at eye level, and restart the explanation slowly.
Give a short break before re-offering medication with a familiar staff member when possible.
Confirm where the walker is before asking her to stand.
Corridor noise near the room can increase agitation.
Unfamiliar staff moving quickly during medication rounds can lead to refusal.
Unexpected walker repositioning can make transfers feel unsafe.

value.situation: Evening medication round during noisy disturbances like fire drills
value.approach: Move resident to a quiet lounge, play favorite music, and offer medication with apple jelly.

citations.quote: She calmed down after staff moved her to the quiet lounge and played her favorite enka music, then took the medication with apple jelly.
She calmed down after staff moved her to the quiet lounge and played her favorite enka music, then took the medication with apple jelly.

handoffBrief.value: Aiko Mori is steady with walker support but slower after lunch; watch for medication refusal tied to unfamiliar staff or corridor noise, and use calm, familiar-nurse re-approaches.

value.claim: Track whether gait slowness appears after lunch or also during morning care.
Watch for repeated medication refusal when unfamiliar staff or corridor noise are present.
Escalate only as an operational handoff concern; avoid diagnostic or prescribing language.
